# The memory of stone and the electronic eye: when the horizon was a threat

The Costa Tropical, that sun-drenched balcony gazing brazenly at the Mediterranean, hides beneath its skin of saltpeter and prickly pears a geography of surveillance. The watchtowers, mute and severe, were not raised for the traveler's delight, but out of an atavistic fear of what the easterly wind might bring. Today, that dread has mutated into binary codes and thermal sensors, yet the instinct to protect the shore remains the same: an invisible line separating our peace from the uncertainty of the great blue.

## The architecture of fear: the language of smoke

The watchtowers that dot our coastline, such as the one at Cambrón or Carchuna, are not monuments to isolation, but nodes in a hyperactive communication network. In the 16th century, the horizon was the stage for a recurring nightmare: the Barbary galleys. The structure of these towers responded to a logical necessity: height and visibility. The watchman, his gaze fixed on the line of the waves, was not waiting to contemplate the sunset, but to detect the glint of a hostile sail. A column of smoke at midday or an orange bonfire in the blackness of the night were the WhatsApp of their time; a direct message to the heart of the castles so that the interior could arm itself before the pirates hit the sand.

## From the lookout’s hollow to the SIVE thermal sensor

The SIVE (Integrated External Surveillance System) is, in essence, the technical evolution of that watchman who, alone in his watchtower, defied sleep. The fundamental difference lies in the nature of perception: where the human eye surrendered to fog or darkness, long-range radar now scrutinizes every centimeter of the sea's surface. There is no magic, only an implacable precision that reaches up to seventy kilometers. The stations installed on the headlands of Granada process the pulse of the sea in real time, translating the movement of any vessel into cold data, stripping the ocean of its romantic halo of mystery to convert it into a gridded map of security.

## The view from the sky: the silent flight of drones

The drone has replaced the messenger on horseback who would gallop from the coast inland with word of an incursion. It is a discreet tool, a mechanical eagle that patrols without raising suspicion, capable of entering remote coves where history has stood still. While the pirates of Algiers relied on the cover of night and the skill of their rowers, the current devices of the Guardia Civil operate with the coldness of a thermal camera. Surveillance has abandoned the stone to rise into the sky; it is no longer a static point that watches, but a mobile eye that neither rests nor blinks, integrating the air and the sea into a single controlled arena.

## Heritage at a crossroads: stones that speak of us

It is ironic to walk by the Torre del Diablo or the remains of the structures in Salobreña and feel the weight of a neglect that seems unjust to us. We have turned defense into cutting-edge technology, but we have let the original support—that stone carved by 16th-century hands—crumble under erosion. These towers are the scars of our history, the marks of a time when life hung in the balance of a nighttime landing. Protecting them is not an act of nostalgia, but a matter of respect for our identity; a recognition that, before radars, we were a people who learned to read danger in fire and in the attentive gaze of a lonely sentinel.

## The persistence of the horizon

The Costa Tropical remains, after all, a borderland. Yesterday, the threat arrived with sabers and captives; today, the dynamics of migratory flows and smuggling pose new challenges for those who guard these waters. What does not change is the immensity of the sea, that surface which reflects both the beauty of our sunsets and the tragedy of those who attempt to cross it. We are children of this coastline, heirs to watchmen and guardians, and as long as the towers remain standing—even if only as skeletons of stone—they remind us that, on this shore, peace is never a natural state, but a constant effort to remain alert to what arrives from the horizon.

Until next time — the Coast awaits you, from Almuñécar to the Axarquía.
